data scientist turned technical
recruiter.
You want to give a quick introduction of
yourself, Donald?
Yes, hello Chad. So I was a data
scientist in Canada for about 5 years. I
worked mainly with insurance companies.
I was building AI models for detecting
insurance fraud.
And
after 3 years at my most recent company,
I I sort of stopped learning to an
extent and I wanted something new. I
also wanted something more
people-focused.
And so I tried to apply to different
positions that were kind of adjacent but
people-focused like product manager or
like technical engagement manager.
But as most of you may know, the North
American job market is a struggle right
now, especially if you're not very
senior in your careers.
So my wife who's [screaming] Japanese
recommended moving to Japan. And so we
we came over here to Japan and uh
and I I just started my own company. You
know, I was tired of applying,
recruiters were misrepresenting me. And
so I said, let's just build something of
my own.
And I I saw some success in my own
company. It was basically data science
consulting for some startups and some
larger corporations. At one point I got
a decent-sized project in Japan and I
had to hire some freelancers
who were engineers and I was actually
pretty good at hiring. I made some good
decisions. I also had one mistake that I
made in hiring, but it taught me a lot
about how do you hire the right people
in tech. And so after that project was
finished, I had to make a decision on
what I wanted to do next. And I became
really interested in recruiting as a
result because of how poorly I was
represented in Canada and how I guess
well I thought I was doing in my own

And so now I am a recruiter in Tokyo

What did you have to learn?
What do you have to do day-to-day? How

Yes. So there's two types of recruiters.
There's the what you'd call a
headhunter, which is what I am. You work
for an agency and your agency has
partnerships with several clients and
they ask you to do specific searches
like find me a software engineer with
this skill set. And the nice thing about
working with an agency is you have more
diversity on where you can send people.
So even if they're not a match for one

be a good fit. That's what I do. Then
there's also in-house talent

specific company like let's say Amazon
and you can access all of Amazon's

Amazon as a company.
And so you asked, what have I learned?
A lot, man. Like
